The President of the US talks about 'making Hollywood great again', but the problem isn't investing in the American film industry, it's trying to do it on our dime and against our laws. Threatening Europe's cultural rules is not acceptable. Our laws protect creators, children and independent media. Let me be clear: culture will never, ever be a bargaining chip in any trade negotiation.
President von der Leyen, we need to send a clear message across the Atlantic: Europe will not back down. First, European citizens decide what rules govern their culture. Second, our audiovisual sector as a source of jobs and creativity must be defended. Our sovereignty and credibility are on the line. Culture is key to preserving our diversity and Europe's soft power. The AVMSD is not just a law on paper, it's a shield for Europe's culture. When we updated it in 2018, we made a deliberate choice: protect European works in a fast-changing media world, keep harmful content away from our children and guarantee independent media regulators. Yes, this means big platforms like Netflix or Disney+ must invest in European stories, but it also creates the blockbusters of tomorrow right here in Europe.
No, the AVMSD is not a barrier, it's a shield. No, the AVMSD is not a distortion of trade, it opens doors for European talents. Look at the success of脗聽Borgen, Lupin or Money Heist: these are all European works that have reached global audiences, mostly through big platforms like Netflix. If it was not for the AVMSD, major audiovisual platforms 芒聙聯 which are mainly American 芒聙聯 would probably not invest so much in European productions. Given the power that these platforms wield, we must make sure European stories remain visible in Europe. The sustainability of our audiovisual sector depends on it, as does our cultural diversity.
Mr Trump, attacking European rules to protect Hollywood is hypocritical. If you really care about American creators, meet them, talk to the guilds. They told us last May, when we were in LA, that 100脗聽% tariffs on foreign films is a bad idea. Many American productions export to Europe because of lower costs: it's business, not a culture threat. Art and culture thrive by opening doors, not closing them. European and American cinema have always grown together. Let's keep it that way.
Now, I would like to thank my CULT colleagues for their support and introduce my oral question to the Commission on the AVMSD obligations in the transatlantic dialogue.
The administration of US President Donald Trump has recently targeted as foreign trade barriers the provisions of the Audiovisual Media Services Directive (AVMSD) applying to streaming industries. In particular, it has focussed on investment obligations aimed at promoting European content and cultural diversity and a requirement for the catalogues of providers of on-demand audiovisual media services to meet a quota of at least 30脗聽% of European works and give these works appropriate prominence. Furthermore, within the context of the ongoing trade negotiations, President Trump also stated his intention to impose a 100脗聽% tax on films made outside the United States.
First, the AVMSD is a cornerstone of the EU's audiovisual policy, aiming to create a single market for audiovisual media services in the EU while promoting cultural diversity. Does the Commission consider that backtracking on the principles of the AVMSD would be detrimental to the European culture model given the context of very strong international competition within the audiovisual media sector? And second, is the Commission in favour of recognising the crucial importance of the AVMSD in defending Europe's cultural sovereignty and diversity and the soft power of the EU, both in its negotiation with the United States and also in evaluating the AVMSD prior to its potential review in 2026?

The directive is the long-standing foundation of Europe's audiovisual content legislation. It harmonises the rules for the provision of audiovisual media services across the Union, supporting European creation, and it guarantees that audiovisual service providers active in the EU contribute fairly to the diversity and vitality of our cultural ecosystem.
The EU remains firmly committed to the principles of this directive, as well as to open markets and non-discriminatory laws and taxes in full alignment with global trade rules. Accordingly, the directive applies equally to all audiovisual media service providers operating in our single market, regardless of their country of origin.
Those rules encompass the possibility for Member States to impose financial contributions for the production of European works, both on broadcasters and on on-demand services. This framework has contributed significantly to the objective of promoting cultural and linguistic diversity within the Union.
Next year, the Commission will assess the impact of the directive and present an evaluation report accompanied by proposals for review. Our work will take into account, in particular, the new challenges related to the prominence of European content, of general interest, fair advertising and the emergence of new technologies and players such as influencers. All are crucial issues for the sustainability of the European media sector. Simplification will also be one of our guiding principles in this exercise.
In addition, we will also look at the impact and application of the measures aimed at protecting minors from illegal or harmful content, including on video sharing platforms.

But we also know that our work is far from complete. Building on recent resolve, the Council adopted last week conclusions on violence against women and domestic violence. These conclusions focused on three important pillars: prevention, early detection and intervention. Both the national authorities and the public play a role here, especially in spotting violence earlier, supporting victims and raising the alarm.
Men and boys should also be encouraged to help prevent, detect and intervene against violence. They may also be victims of violence themselves and have gender-specific barriers to reaching out for help.
Honourable Members, in the EU, approximately two women are killed every day by a partner or family member, and gender-based violence costs the EU approximately EUR脗聽290脗聽billion each year in social benefits and lost labour. Yet we still have many who believe that we already have achieved gender equality. Violence against women is rooted in old social attitudes, gender stereotypes and restrictive social norms.
Stopping it requires efforts on many fronts. Action at the level of Member States is key, but the EU can also make very important contributions. The work involves many different areas of life.
One is the digital world. New forms of violence are starting online, including through the misuse of AI. We must adapt our policies to this. This includes the non-consensual sharing of intimate or manipulated images, cyber stalking, cyber harassment, and incitement to violence and hatred. In an equal society, everyone, including women and girls, should feel safe to take part in the digital world. We must protect women and girls against these risks. We have made historic progress in the past five years: the EU accession to the Istanbul Convention was the first step. The EU is now bound by the highest international standards on combating violence against women. The second major step was the adoption of the first-ever EU law on combating violence against women and domestic violence.
We thank the European Parliament once again for the crucial role you played in the negotiations. Your strong dedication and spirit of constructive compromise made this long-awaited result possible. This will also put mothers more at ease in these difficult situations. The directive also criminalises certain forms of violence at EU level 芒聙聯 including forced marriage, which is a form of sexual exploitation of the woman or girl.脗聽To help women and mothers to escape violent relationships and start a new life for themselves and their children, we must promote women's economic empowerment. Gender equality policies help women and mothers to become financially independent.
Our achievements over the past years are not the end of our work on gender-based violence. As long as the headlines continue to report cases of femicide, domestic violence and sexual violence, we will not rest. That is why, in March, the Commission presented the EU Roadmap for Women's Rights, reaffirming the EU's commitment to advance gender equality and women's rights. The roadmap will underpin concrete policy initiatives that will be defined in the next gender equality strategy. One week ago, Commissioner Habib hosted an event in Brussels to celebrate the broad political support that the Roadmap for Women's Rights received from European political, institutional and civil society leaders. The European Parliament, the Economic and Social Committee, the Committee of Regions, the European Investment Bank and all 27 Member States endorsed the roadmap's Declaration of principles for a gender-equal society.
We are all committed to the same guiding principles in our future actions. The very first principle in the Declaration of principles is freedom from gender-based violence. Only by being free from violence can women enjoy and thrive in all aspects of their lives. To further empower women and their families to make life choices that reflect their aspirations, it is also key to advance the other principles of the roadmap's Declaration of principles. It is essential to ensure that every woman has the right to the highest attainable standards of physical and mental health, the right to equal pay for equal work, the right to equal employment opportunities and adequate working conditions, the right to balance their professional and private life, the right to high quality and inclusive education and training, the right to actively and safely participate in public life 芒聙聯 to mention just a few of those rights.
We've already made some steps in these directions with the legislation and policy initiatives adopted in recent years. Now we need to make sure that these legislations become a reality on the ground in all Member States. Only then will women and men equally thrive in our societies.

To address this, we must stay firm and determined in tackling the root causes of gender-based violence. Fighting gender-based violence is not just a women's issue, it is a societal issue. It affects our communities, our economies and our democracies. It undermines the values we hold dear: equality, justice and freedom.
Political and spiritual leader Bacha Khan once said, 'If you wish to know how civilised the culture is, look at how they treat their women.' If one third of EU women or girls live in fear of violence, we are failing as a society. This simply cannot be our Union of equality.
Together with your contribution and that of the other EU institutions, we must build a Europe where every woman can live free from fear and where equality is not a promise but a reality.

We need men in this debate. Men and boys have to be part of the debate. Many men want to help prevent, detect and intervene against violence: we have to encourage that. And an aspect which we, as women, also have to take into account is that men and boys may also be victims of violence themselves, and it is not easy for them to reach out for help because of gender-specific barriers to them.
A part of the debate has also been the consequences of migration. In my mind, there can be no doubt that that is also part of the debate, at least for my own country. Immigrants from certain parts of the world have higher criminal rates, and women with immigrant background are over-represented in women's shelters. The lack of gender equality in immigrant environments is a major gender equality problem.
I also got a question about surrogacy. Surrogacy is within the remit of national law. This is also clearly the approach taken in the Directive on combating human trafficking, which only deals with the exploitation of surrogacy, not surrogacy itself.
We can be proud of what we have achieved in the European Union: we are the most gender-equal place in the world. But we need to look ahead: we do not have gender equality. The Council looks forward to the new gender equality strategy that the Commission is expected to issue soon. Meanwhile, Member States continue their work at national level.
We should see this as an opportunity: promoting gender equality policies also gives us a way to strengthen the cohesion, resilience and competitiveness of our societies.

As you know, the system with the seasonal change was introduced by many Member States in the 1970s as a response to the energy crisis, with the goal of saving energy. But as Europe grew closer and the internal market took shape, a patchwork of different summer times, start and end dates, across Member States, became a real challenge, particularly for the transport and energy costs and sectors. That is why it was decided to synchronise these dates to bring order to the EU internal market.
But times have changed, honourable Members. With today's technology and digitalisation, this system no longer produces any energy savings, not for any sector. The seasonal time change has become a source of unnecessary complication for society.
Take the transport sector, for example. Operators have to adjust departure and arrival times twice a year, often at the cost of confusion and frustration for passengers. And, of course, some of you might think: 'When I travel between the European Parliament and my home country every week, I also change time zones. So what is the difference?' Although true for some, this is not the daily reality for the EU citizens. Of course, anyone can choose to travel for work or leisure across time zones, but no one should be forced by law to experience a clock change in their daily lives, especially when we know 芒聙聯 we know it 芒聙聯 that it harms people's health.
Research shows that these forced clock changes disrupt our inner body clock, interfering with our sleep routine and our metabolism. In the short term, they reduce our mood, energy and alertness, which already lowers productivity at work and road safety. But over the long term, the effects can be far more serious, with a higher risk of different illnesses. These negative effects impact everyone, but children and the elderly are especially vulnerable and their voices are rarely heard. Yet they are the ones that suffer more from these seasonal clock changes.
We have, honourable Members of the European Parliament, the responsibility and the possibility to act now. When the Commission asked Europeans for their views, a record-breaking 4.6脗聽million citizens responded 芒聙聯 the highest participation rate ever in a Commission public consultation. The citizen's message was loud and clear: 84脗聽% want us to end this absurdity of changing the clocks twice a year.
That is why the Commission put forward a proposal to end the seasonal time change, and I am glad that the European Parliament supported this vision and voted in favour to abolish the time change. However, the Member States in the Council have not yet reached a unified position, so our work must continue.
From day one of this new Commission, I have been engaging closely, as the Commissioner responsible for this file, with Council presidencies and Member States to carefully examine the legal and practical implications of clock change. Following discussions initiated by the Polish Presidency of the Council, the Commission has decided to undertake further analysis through a detailed study that we are working on right now to support future decision-making on this issue, so I look forward to sharing the results with you 芒聙聯 and, of course, with the Member States 芒聙聯 as soon as they are available. I also welcome that Spain raised this point at the Energy Council meeting earlier this week, showing their motivation to move forward, and that several Member States are today supportive.
The people of Europe want us to act, and my goal is to move forward. The time has come to finally stop the seasonal changes of time every year. The Commission is fully committed to working hand in hand with Parliament and the Council to deliver a pragmatic, timely solution that benefits all Europeans. So I really look forward to a lively and constructive debate today.
